title: The Movie of Surah Yusuf: From the Pit to the Throne
oneLineSpine: A boy dreams eleven stars bow to him, is thrown into a well by jealous brothers, sold into Egypt, jailed for refusing sin, freed by reading the king's dream, raised to power, and finally reunited with the very family that betrayed him — who bow before him exactly as the dream foretold.

acts.name: Overture: The Best of Stories
acts.summary: Alif-Laam-Ra. These are the verses of the clear Book, revealed as an Arabic Quran so you may understand. Allah announces He will relate to Muhammad 'the best of stories' (ahsan al-qasas) — a story the Prophet had been unaware of before.
acts.emotionalBeat: Anticipation — Allah Himself promises 'the BEST story.' The learner leans in before a single event happens.
acts.sceneImage: A darkened cinema; a single spotlight hits a clear open Book on a stand, and a narrator's voice says 'Watch. This is the finest story ever told.'

acts.name: The Dream of Eleven Stars
acts.summary: Young Yusuf tells his father Yaqub: 'O my father, I saw eleven stars, the sun and the moon — I saw them prostrating to me.' Yaqub warns him not to tell his brothers lest Shaytan plot against him, and foretells that Allah will choose him and teach him the interpretation of dreams (ta'wil al-ahadith).
acts.emotionalBeat: Intimate wonder — a child's secret shared with a loving father, shadowed by the father's quiet dread of what's coming.
acts.sceneImage: A boy at his father's knee under a night sky where eleven stars plus the sun and moon literally tip and bow toward him.

acts.name: The Brothers' Jealousy and the Plot
acts.summary: The brothers grumble: 'Yusuf and his brother are more beloved to our father than us, though we are a strong group.' They plot: 'Kill Yusuf or cast him to some land so our father's face is free for us.' One softer voice says: don't kill him — throw him into the bottom of the well (ghayabat al-jubb), and some caravan will pick him up.
acts.emotionalBeat: Cold dread — love turned to murderous envy; the chilling calm with which brothers debate a sibling's death.
acts.sceneImage: A huddle of grown brothers in a field, one pointing down at a dark stone well, the word 'well' hanging in the air like a verdict.

acts.name: The Deception at the Door
acts.summary: The brothers coax Yaqub: 'Why don't you trust us with Yusuf? Send him with us tomorrow to play; we will surely guard him.' Yaqub replies it grieves him and he fears a wolf (dhi'b) will eat him while they are heedless. They scoff: 'If the wolf ate him while we are a strong group, we would truly be losers.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Sickening dramatic irony — the father names the exact lie (the wolf) they will later use, and hands over his son anyway.
acts.sceneImage: A doorway at dawn; smiling brothers leading a small boy by the hand, while a wolf's shadow flickers unnoticed on the wall.

acts.name: The Pit and the False Shirt
acts.summary: They take him and agree to drop him into the well; Allah inspires Yusuf: 'You will surely inform them of this affair while they do not perceive.' They come to their father at night weeping, claiming a wolf ate Yusuf while they raced — and bring his shirt (qamees) stained with false blood. Yaqub sees through it: 'Beautiful patience (sabr jameel); Allah is the One whose help is sought.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Heartbreak meets steel — a father's grief, but transmuted into 'sabr jameel,' the surah's emotional keynote.
acts.sceneImage: A boy vanishing into a black well; then a bloodied white shirt held up before an old man whose face hardens into quiet resolve.

acts.name: The Caravan and the Cheap Sale
acts.summary: A caravan (sayyara) arrives; their water-drawer lowers his bucket and cries 'Good news! A boy!' They hide him as merchandise and sell him for a paltry price, a few counted dirhams, being of those uninterested in him. Allah is All-Knowing of what they do.
acts.emotionalBeat: Bitter smallness — a future king traded for pocket change; the world underestimates what Allah is elevating.
acts.sceneImage: A bucket rising from the well with a boy clinging to the rope; then a hand counting a tiny pile of silver coins.

acts.name: The House of al-Aziz
acts.summary: The man of Egypt who bought him tells his wife: 'Make his stay honorable; perhaps he will benefit us or we take him as a son.' Thus Allah established Yusuf in the land and taught him interpretation of events — 'Allah is predominant over His affair, but most people do not know.' When Yusuf reached maturity, Allah gave him wisdom and knowledge.
acts.emotionalBeat: Quiet reversal — the sold slave becomes an honored ward; the reader feels Allah's hidden hand steering.
acts.sceneImage: A grand Egyptian household's gate opening; a nobleman gesturing to his wife to welcome the boy into a home of marble and light.

acts.name: The Temptation and 'Ma'adhallah'
acts.summary: The woman in whose house he lived tried to seduce him, bolted the doors, and said 'Come, you!' He answered 'Ma'adhallah — I seek refuge in Allah; indeed my master honored my stay; the wrongdoers never succeed.' She desired him and he would have inclined but for seeing the proof of his Lord (burhan rabbihi); thus Allah turned evil and immorality from His sincere servant.
acts.emotionalBeat: Moral peak — the whole surah's spine of virtue: temptation at full force, defeated by God-consciousness in an instant.
acts.sceneImage: Bolted doors from the inside; a young man's hand raised in refusal, a beam of light ('the proof') cutting across the dim room.

acts.name: The Race to the Door and the Torn Shirt
acts.summary: They both race to the door; she tears his shirt from the back (min dubur) and they find her husband at the door. She accuses Yusuf. A witness from her family reasons: if the shirt is torn from the front she's truthful, if from the back she lied. Seeing it torn from the back, the husband declares: 'This is of your (women's) plot (kayd) — mighty is your plot. Yusuf, ignore this; and you (woman), seek forgiveness for your sin.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Vindication by evidence — truth is proven by a torn garment; the forensic 'shirt' motif returns as Yusuf's defense.
acts.sceneImage: Two figures sprinting at a door that swings open; a shirt ripping at the back seam; a husband inspecting the tear like a judge.

acts.name: The Banquet and the Cut Hands
acts.summary: City women gossip that al-Aziz's wife chases her slave-boy. She invites them, gives each a knife, and says 'Come out to them.' Seeing Yusuf they are so awestruck they cut their hands (qatta'na aydiyahunna), crying 'This is no man — this is a noble angel!' She admits she sought him and threatens prison. Yusuf prays: 'My Lord, prison is dearer to me than what they call me to.' Allah answers and turns their plot away.
acts.emotionalBeat: Stunned admiration turned to defiant piety — Yusuf would rather be jailed than sin; principle over comfort.
acts.sceneImage: A banquet of women, citrus and knives in hand, all bleeding from distracted fingers as a radiant youth steps through the door.

acts.name: Into Prison: The Two Dreamers
acts.summary: Despite seeing the signs, they jail him 'for a time.' Two young men enter with him: one dreams of pressing wine, the other of birds eating bread off his head. Yusuf first preaches tawhid — 'Are many lords better, or Allah the One, the Irresistible?' — then interprets: one will serve wine to his master, the other will be crucified and birds will eat from his head. He asks the saved one to mention him to the king, but Shaytan makes him forget, so Yusuf stays years more.
acts.emotionalBeat: Faith amid injustice — the prisoner turns his cell into a pulpit, then is forgotten; hope deferred stretches for years.
acts.sceneImage: A stone cell with two men describing dreams — one squeezing grapes, the other with birds pecking bread off his head.

acts.name: The King's Dream and the Recall
acts.summary: The king dreams of seven fat cows eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and seven dry. His court calls it confused dreams. The freed cupbearer remembers Yusuf. Yusuf interprets: seven years of farming, then seven hard years, then a year of relief. The king summons him; Yusuf insists his innocence be established first. The women confess: 'Now the truth is manifest' — al-Aziz's wife admits she seduced him and 'he is of the truthful.' Yusuf reflects: the soul commands evil, except by Allah's mercy.
acts.emotionalBeat: Long-awaited justice — the man wrongly jailed refuses freedom until his name is cleared; truth finally surfaces publicly.
acts.sceneImage: Seven plump cows being devoured by seven skeletal cows; then a courtroom where a woman finally says aloud, 'The truth is now clear.'

acts.name: The Rise to Power
acts.summary: The king says 'Bring him — I reserve him for myself,' and after speaking with Yusuf declares him 'established and trusted.' Yusuf asks: 'Appoint me over the treasuries of the land; I am a knowing guardian (hafiz alim).' Thus Allah established Yusuf in the land to settle wherever he willed. 'We do not waste the reward of the doers of good' — and the reward of the Hereafter is better.
acts.emotionalBeat: Triumphant vindication — the pit-boy now controls Egypt's granaries; the dream's climb begins to complete.
acts.sceneImage: A former prisoner standing atop the royal treasury steps, keys to Egypt's storehouses of grain resting in his hands.

acts.name: The Brothers Return; the Held-Back Brother
acts.summary: Famine drives Yusuf's brothers to Egypt for grain; he recognizes them but they don't know him. He demands they bring their half-brother next time, secretly returns their goods, then keeps the brother by hiding the king's measuring-cup in his bag, so it appears the boy 'stole.' The brothers plead, then are told to go back and tell their father his son stole — but they insist they only testified to what they knew and were not guardians of the unseen.
acts.emotionalBeat: Suspenseful reversal of power — the betrayed brother now holds all the cards, testing the very men who sold him.
acts.sceneImage: Grain sacks in a busy Egyptian storehouse; a royal golden cup being quietly slipped into a young man's saddlebag.

acts.name: The Blind Father's Grief and 'Sabr Jameel' Again
acts.summary: Yaqub again says 'Beautiful patience (sabr jameel); perhaps Allah will bring them all to me.' He turns away crying 'O my grief for Yusuf,' and his eyes turn white from sorrow, suppressing his anguish. He says 'I only complain of my grief to Allah, and I know from Allah what you do not know,' and sends his sons: 'Go and seek news of Yusuf and his brother, and never despair of the mercy (rawh) of Allah.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Devastating endurance rewarded by hope — a father weeps himself blind yet refuses despair; the surah's greatest lesson in trust.
acts.sceneImage: An old man, eyes gone milky-white with weeping, turning his face to the sky and whispering his complaint to God alone.

acts.name: The Great Reveal: 'I am Yusuf'
acts.summary: The brothers return in poverty begging charity. Yusuf asks: 'Do you know what you did to Yusuf and his brother in your ignorance?' They gasp: 'Are you really Yusuf?' He says 'I am Yusuf, and this is my brother; Allah has favored us. Whoever fears Allah and is patient — Allah does not waste the reward of the good.' They confess sin; he answers with the surah's most merciful line: 'No blame on you today; may Allah forgive you.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Overwhelming catharsis and mercy — the moment of recognition, then radical forgiveness instead of revenge.
acts.sceneImage: A ruler removing all disguise, leaning forward to say 'I am Yusuf' — and then, hands open, 'No blame on you today.'

acts.name: The Healing Shirt and the Scent of Yusuf
acts.summary: Yusuf sends his shirt (qamees) to be cast on his father's face to restore his sight. As the caravan departs, Yaqub says 'I smell the scent of Yusuf.' When the bearer of glad tidings casts the shirt on his face, his sight returns; Yaqub says 'Did I not tell you I know from Allah what you do not know?' The sons beg forgiveness, and he promises to seek it from his Lord.
acts.emotionalBeat: Miraculous restoration — the same garment motif that once carried a lie now carries healing; grief reversed to joy.
acts.sceneImage: A shirt sailing through the air onto a blind old man's face — and his white, sightless eyes flooding back to clear vision.

acts.name: The Prostration: The Dream Fulfilled
acts.summary: The family enters Egypt in safety; Yusuf raises his parents onto the throne and they fall down in prostration (sujjadan) to him. He says: 'O my father, this is the interpretation of my dream from before; my Lord made it true.' He recounts Allah's kindness — bringing him from prison and them from the desert after Shaytan sowed discord — then prays: 'Cause me to die as a Muslim and join me with the righteous.'
acts.emotionalBeat: Full-circle awe — the eleven stars, sun and moon of Act 2 now literally bow; a lifetime of patience crowned, ending in humble prayer.
acts.sceneImage: Eleven brothers plus mother and father bowing before an enthroned Yusuf — the night-sky dream now made flesh in a throne room.

acts.name: Epilogue: This Is No Invented Tale
acts.summary: Allah tells Muhammad this is unseen news revealed to him; most people will not believe however eager he is, though he asks no reward — it is but a reminder to the worlds. Signs are ignored, most who believe still associate partners. 'Say: this is my way; I call to Allah with insight.' When messengers nearly despaired, Allah's help came. In these stories is a lesson (ibrah) for people of understanding — not invented, but confirmation, detail, guidance, and mercy for believers.
acts.emotionalBeat: Sober resolve — the curtain lifts from the movie to address the viewer: this really happened, and it is guidance for you.
acts.sceneImage: The story-film freezes; the narrator turns to the camera and says, 'This was no invented tale — it is a lesson for those with minds.'

memoryHooks: THE SHIRT (qamees) appears three times as a spine-thread: (1) the FALSE bloodied shirt that deceives Yaqub [18], (2) the TORN-from-behind shirt that proves Yusuf's innocence [25-28], (3) the HEALING shirt that restores Yaqub's sight [93,96]. Lie -> Proof -> Healing. Track the shirt and you track the plot.

DREAMS bookend and drive everything: Yusuf's star dream [4] opens the arc, the two prisoners' dreams [36] pivot him toward power, the king's cow dream [43] frees him, and the star dream's FULFILLMENT [100] closes the loop. Four dreams; the first and last are the same dream.

'SABR JAMEEL' (beautiful patience) is Yaqub's twice-spoken refrain [18 and 83], both times after being handed a lie ('sawwalat lakum anfusukum amran' — 'your souls enticed you' — is repeated verbatim in both verses). The repeated sentence marks both grief-scenes and links Act 5 to Act 15.

MIRROR STRUCTURE (ring composition): the surah opens with betrayal at HOME (brothers, father, the pit) and closes with reunion at HOME; the dream at the start is answered by the prostration at the end; Yusuf goes DOWN into the well then DOWN into prison, then UP to the treasury then UP onto the throne. Down-down-up-up.

KAYD (plot/scheme) is the recurring danger-word: the brothers' feared kayd [5], the women's kayd [28,33,34,50], and the ordained plan. Wherever you see 'kayd,' someone is scheming against Yusuf — and Allah always overturns it.

The NUMBER SEVEN clusters in the king's dream and its interpretation: seven fat cows, seven lean, seven green ears, seven dry, seven good years, seven hard years [43,46,47,48]. Seven is the granary-famine act's signature number.
'INNAHU LA YUDEE'U AJRA AL-MUHSINEEN' / rewarding the good-doers echoes across the rises [22, 56, 90] — the divine payoff line that recurs each time Yusuf is elevated.

TA'WIL AL-AHADITH (interpretation of events/dreams) is promised by Yaqub in Act 2 [6], granted in the house of al-Aziz [21], exercised in prison [36-41] and before the king [43-49], and acknowledged in Yusuf's closing prayer [101]. The gift named at the start is the engine of the whole rise.

REPEATED CALL 'ya sahibayi as-sijn' (O my two prison-companions) [39,41] tags the prison da'wah scene; repeated 'i'tuni bihi' (bring him to me) [50,54] tags the king's summons scene — recurring phrases act as scene-labels.
pairingStrategy: Anchor every atomic word-cloze MCQ to a numbered ACT before it is ever drilled, so the learner recalls a word by its LOCATION in the movie rather than as a free-floating card. Concretely: (1) Tag each word MCQ with its act number and sceneImage, and surface that scene image as the MCQ's visual/context header — e.g. a cloze on 'qamees' always shows which of the three shirt-scenes it belongs to, forcing the learner to place it on the shirt-thread. (2) Sequence delivery act-by-act, not ayah-by-ayah in isolation: complete an act's scene narration first, then drill that act's high-value words (the verbs and nouns that carry the plot: alqoohu/ghayabat al-jubb for the pit, raawadat-hu/ma'adhallah for the temptation, qadd/dubur for the torn shirt, saba' baqarat for the king's dream), so each word attaches to a live mental image. (3) Exploit the memory hooks as cross-act linking decks: run a dedicated 'shirt' set (the three qamees ayahs together), a 'dreams' set (4, 36, 43, 100 together), and a 'sabr jameel' pair (18 vs 83, contrasting the identical 'sawwalat lakum anfusukum' wording) — these teach recurring vocabulary through their pattern, which is far stickier than isolated repetition. (4) Use the ring/mirror structure for retrieval scaffolding: pair beginning-act words with their end-act echoes (star-dream vocabulary in Act 2 paired with prostration vocabulary in Act 18; 'kayd' when feared by brothers paired with 'kayd' of the women) so recalling one half cues the other. (5) For spaced repetition, let the one-line spine be the top-level index: the learner mentally walks the 19 acts in order, and each act 'opens' to reveal its word-cloze cards — turning review into re-watching the movie scene by scene, which keeps recall structured, ordered, and long-retained rather than a shuffled flashcard pile.
